title: Jilly and Mia Workbench Console
summary: A long rolling console styled after an industrial workbench, with notched 2x4 legs threaded onto a through-stretcher, a slatted 1x6 lower shelf, and a 2x10 plank top.
description:
The signature joint here is the notched leg: each of the eight leg pieces gets a 3/4"-deep notch cut with repeated circular-saw passes and cleaned out with a chisel, so the middle stretcher passes THROUGH the legs rather than butting against them. That's what gives this piece its exposed-joinery workbench look, and it's also what makes the whole base rock-solid once the stretcher is screwed and glued into every notch.

Decorative 3" lag screws with exposed heads reinforce the show-face joints for the look, not the strength — the actual holding power comes from the screws and glue underneath. Add the casters last, after the base is fully assembled and square, since a rolling base is much harder to true up than a stationary one.

tools.note: Cleaning out the notches after the circular saw passes.
Notching the end shelf boards to fit around the legs.

materials.name: 3" lag screws
materials.note: Decorative exposed heads at the notched joints.

cutList.part: Bottom shelf aprons, front and back

steps.title: Cut all the parts
steps.body: Cut everything before you assemble anything, squaring every cut. From the eight 2x4s: four longer leg pieces at 34-1/2", four shorter leg pieces at 31", two top aprons at 88-1/2", five top studs at 20-3/4", two center legs at 31", and two inside center legs at 22". From the three 2x6s: two bottom shelf aprons at 88-1/2", two middle leg stretchers at 22-1/4", and two shorter bottom stretchers at 13-3/4". From the four 1x6s cut fifteen bottom shelf boards at 20-3/4", and from the two 2x2s cut two shelf board supports at 81". The three 2x10s are used at their full 8 ft length as the top — do not cut them. Allowing 1/8" for each saw kerf, all eight 2x4s get used, so cut the two 88-1/2" aprons first, one to its own board, and take the shorter parts out of what is left.

steps.title: Notch the leg pieces
steps.body: Each leg is two boards face to face — a 34-1/2" longer piece and a 31" shorter piece — and each gets a notch so the middle 2x6 stretcher passes through the pair rather than butting into it. Make the notch 5-1/2" wide to match the 2x6's width and 3/4" deep in each board, so the two 3/4" notches together form the 1-1/2" pocket the stretcher's thickness needs. Set the circular saw's blade depth to 3/4", make repeated kerf cuts across the notch area roughly every 1/4", then knock out and pare the waste with a chisel until the floor of the notch is flat and the stretcher slides in snugly. Clamp the two boards of a leg together and lay out both notches at once so they line up; a stretcher that meets two offset notches will twist the leg.

steps.title: Assemble the leg sets around the middle stretcher
steps.body: Slide a 22-1/4" middle stretcher into the notches of each leg set so it passes clean through, and clamp the two leg boards tight against it. Fasten with glue and 2-1/2" screws driven from the inside, starting in the longer 34-1/2" leg piece so the heads stay off the show face. Check the leg set for square before the glue grabs — a 13-3/4" shorter bottom stretcher laid in dry makes a good spacer to true it up. Drive the 3" lag screws through the show face into the stretcher at this joint if you want the exposed heads; they are decoration, so set them evenly rather than wherever they land.

steps.title: Attach the bottom stretcher
steps.body: Attach the shorter bottom stretcher with 1-1/2" pocket holes and 2-1/2" pocket hole screws. If you don't have a pocket hole jig, screw in at an angle instead, or notch it the same way as the middle stretcher. Drill the pocket holes at 3/4" rather than 1-1/2" so the screw doesn't land in the gap between the two leg boards.

steps.title: Attach the top leg piece
steps.body: Attach the top 2x4 in place with 2-1/2" screws and glue. This completes the two leg sets.

steps.title: Add the top studs
steps.body: Stand the two 88-1/2" top aprons parallel and run the five 20-3/4" top studs between them, ends flush with the outside faces of the aprons so the frame comes out 20-3/4" deep. Space the studs evenly along the 88-1/2" run — that works out near 18" apart — and keep every stud square to the aprons, since these are what the tabletop lands on. Two 2-1/2" screws through the outside of each apron into each stud end is enough.

steps.title: Attach the center legs
steps.body: Attach the center legs to the top and bottom aprons with 2-1/2" wood screws and glue.

steps.title: Fill the void behind the center leg
steps.body: Fill the void behind the center leg with the smaller inside center leg piece.

steps.title: Add the shelf board supports
steps.body: Screw the two 81" 2x2 supports to the inside faces of the 5-1/2" bottom shelf aprons, one front and one back, set down about 3/4" from the top edge so the 1x6 shelf boards drop in flush with the apron tops. Run them level with each other end to end — check with a straightedge rather than by eye, because any dip here shows up as a rocking shelf board.

steps.title: Lay the bottom shelf boards
steps.body: Lay the fifteen 20-3/4" 1x6 shelf boards across the supports, starting at the center and working outward to each end so any width discrepancy lands at the ends where it is least visible. The boards at each end have to be notched around the legs — hold each one in place, scribe the leg profile directly onto it, and cut to the line with a jigsaw rather than measuring, since the notch has to match the leg as built. Glue each board to the supports and set it with 1-1/4" finish nails.

steps.title: Attach the tabletop
steps.body: Lay the three full-length 9-1/4" wide 2x10s across the frame, edges tight together — that makes a top 27-3/4" deep over a 20-3/4" deep stud frame, so 3-1/2" of overhang to split evenly front and back. Center the 96" top on the base so the two end overhangs match, then glue and screw down into every stud and both top aprons with 2-1/2" screws. Work from the center board outward so the two outer boards can be nudged to keep the joints tight.

steps.title: Attach the casters and finish
steps.body: With the base fully assembled and checked square, tip the console over and screw a caster to the bottom of each leg, centered on the 3-1/2" leg face. Do this last, as the description says — a rolling base is far harder to true up than a stationary one. Sand every surface and ease the edges, then finish as desired.

images.alt: A long rolling console table with notched leg joinery styled after an industrial workbench
